executive_summary:
The current landscape of Staphylococcus aureus dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) resistance includes several well‑characterized mutations that compromise trimethoprim efficacy, yet none of the variants identified in the present GNN analysis have been reported in clinical isolates to date. Consequently, there is no established clinical threat associated with these specific substitutions, but their emergence would represent a novel resistance mechanism.

Among the three candidate mutations, T46Q (Rank 1) presents the greatest potential risk. It exhibits the most pronounced binding disruption (Δ = ‑1.3337 kcal mol⁻¹) while maintaining protein stability within the acceptable threshold (Δ = ‑0.5043, > ‑1.0). This combination of high drug‑binding perturbation and retained enzymatic fitness positions T46Q as the most dangerous novel threat, surpassing the conservative D27E and T46S variants in both potency and survivability.

Continued surveillance for the T46Q substitution in clinical and environmental S. aureus isolates is recommended. Early detection will enable proactive mitigation strategies, including the design of next‑generation DHFR inhibitors that accommodate this radical side‑chain change without compromising potency.
